ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for Kozhi Rasam. A0CKP (chicken soup/broth) is the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kozhi Rasam is approximately 200 ml (roughly 200 g), typically served as a broth accompaniment alongside rice or consumed as a medicinal drink.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Kozhi Rasam exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for chicken broth, rasam, and individual spice components. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken broth, entry 06172) was used as a secondary cross-reference for protein, fat, and energy values. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate for a traditional South Indian chicken rasam preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on chicken broth / low-carbohydrate spiced broth
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kozhi Rasam. GI estimated at approximately 20 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate chicken broth and spiced rasam preparations. The very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g) and high protein content (6.8 g/100 g) strongly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (20 × 2.8 × 2) / 100 = 1.12, rounded to 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 240 mg per 100 g; a 200 ml serving contributes approximately 480 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and limit added salt during preparation, as a single 200 ml serving provides approximately 480 mg sodium, representing roughly 21% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~20) and low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g) with high protein (6.8 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kozhi Rasam is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~20), low carbohydrate load (approximately 5.6 g per 200 ml serving), and high protein content that promotes satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g), cardioprotective spices (black pepper, garlic, ginger, turmeric), and lean protein from chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kozhi Rasam provides lean protein with low saturated fat and contains cardioprotective bioactive compounds from black pepper, garlic, ginger, and turmeric; however, sodium intake should be managed carefully in individuals with cardiovascular disease or heart failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices including ginger, garlic, and black pepper
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory properties of ginger, garlic, and black pepper in Kozhi Rasam may provide symptomatic benefit for rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with acid sensitivity should note that tamarind may exacerbate gastric discomfort.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kozhi Rasam is a traditional South Indian spiced chicken broth originating from Tamil and Chettinad culinary traditions. It is prepared by simmering country chicken (nattu kozhi) with tamarind water, black pepper, cumin, garlic, ginger, tomato, curry leaves, and turmeric, followed by a tempering of mustard seeds and dried red chillies in oil. The result is a thin, intensely flavoured, tangy, and peppery broth with a deep amber colour. Kozhi Rasam is widely regarded as a home remedy for colds, coughs, body aches, and respiratory congestion due to the combined medicinal properties of its spice blend. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate, low-calorie broth providing approximately 60 kcal per 100 g, with 6.8 g protein, 2.5 g fat, and only 2.8 g carbohydrate. Its very low glycemic index (~20) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (240 mg/100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. It is typically served as an accompaniment to rice at lunch or dinner, or consumed as a standalone medicinal drink. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is Kozhi Rasam good for colds?
Is Kozhi Rasam good for diabetes?
Is Kozhi Rasam high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Kozhi Rasam exists in ICMR-NIN published tables. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken broth and rasam component data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken broth (entry 06172). GI is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate broth data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN data for chicken broth and rasam components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken broth, entry 06172). No single approved-source entry exactly matches Kozhi Rasam. 2. cholesterol_mg is set to -1: specific cholesterol data for this composite traditional preparation (country chicken simmered with tamarind and spices) is not individually reported in ICMR-NIN tables or USDA SR Legacy entry 06172 for a Kozhi Rasam preparation; the composite cooking method and variable chicken-to-broth ratio make a defensible single value unavailable from approved sources as of 2024. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific traditional preparation is not reported as a discrete value in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy for a composite Kozhi Rasam; water content varies substantially with preparation method and broth concentration and cannot be reliably assigned from available approved-source data. 4. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Kozhi Rasam was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 5. Saturated fat (0.7 g), monounsaturated fat (1.0 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.5 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy chicken broth fatty acid profile adapted proportionally to the fat content reported. 6. The portions array in the input contained three identical duplicate entries (200 ml, 200 g); deduplicated to one canonical serving. 7. This dish is classified as Indian/Tamil/Chettinad cuisine; multilingual G6 requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Hindi) do not apply. Tamil local name கோழி இரசம் is provided as the primary mandatory local-language name for this South Indian Tamil dish.
